I was looking at the JNI code for the java api (in Native.cpp) and noticed a number of calls to `GetStringUTFChars` without corresponding calls to `ReleaseStringUTFChars`. At best that will result in a memory leak. I haven't tracked down the use of the resulting allocated array, but there is no guarantee that the JVM returns a piece of memory obtained from `malloc`, thus you cannot safely free the memory. Furthermore, if the JVM is running with native memory tracking turned on, then the memory allocated by `GetStringUTFChars` will not be safe to pass to `free`.

issue_title: [CMake] _TRACE is not defined in a Debug build
issue_body:
@NikolajBjorner mentioned this issue to me recently. The Python build system defines ``_TRACE`` in a Debug build but the CMake build system does not.

This was a deliberate decision on my part and I want to explain the rationale:

The CMake build system provides the option ``ENABLE_TRACING`` which is off by default. When this option is enabled ``_TRACE`` is added as a compiler definition. This option is currently independent of the build type (e.g. ``Release``, ``Debug``).

It is not always possible to know the build configuration (e.g. ``Debug``, ``Release``) when CMake is run due to multi-configuration generators (e.g. Visual Studio). This means it is not possible force ``ENABLE_TRACING`` to ``ON`` when doing a debug build. However it is possible (using something CMake calls "generator expressions") to have the ``_TRACE`` define to be added depending to build configuration. The code for this already there but it is commented out

```cmake
# Should we always enable tracing when doing a debug build?
#list(APPEND Z3_COMPONENT_CXX_DEFINES $<$<CONFIG:Debug>:_TRACE>)
```

If uncommented this code it would lead to confusing behaviour because tracing would be enabled in a debug build even if ``ENABLE_TRACING`` is set to ``OFF``. This is why the code is currently commented out.

I can fix this but first I need to know

@NikolajBjorner @wintersteiger 
Is it ever desirable to have a debug build without tracing?

If the answer is yes then I think I should leave the CMake build system as it is to allow building a debug build without tracing. If the answer is no then I think I should rename the CMake option to ``ENABLE_TRACING_IN_NON_DEBUG_BUILD``. Then I would then set the build system to always define ``_TRACE`` in a debug build. The ``ENABLE_TRACING_IN_NON_DEBUG_BUILD`` would no longer be confusing because it would only matter in non debug builds.

issue_title: Low priority feature request: make `get-value` work with Pareto fronts in SMTLib mode
issue_body:
This is a _low-priority_ request.

As discussed in #1001, the `get-value` based interface currently doesn't work for retrieving model-values for Pareto fronts. The current work around is to use:

```
    (set-option :opt.print_model true)
```

to bring out those models. This is less than ideal as it does not allow for precise control of how model values are queried. It is also quite different from how boxed objectives can be retrieved where a `model-index` can be passed, as discussed in https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/issues/955. 

It would be nice if Pareto models can be queried via calls to `get-value`; if at all possible. Perhaps via a `(next-pareto-front)` like directive to move to the next front till we exhaust the pareto fronts.

issue_title: Should a Layer remember how its parameters were initialized?
issue_body:
I have a feature request / idea, and I would be willing to do the implementation. 

The main use case is saving and loading architecture definitions. I have a small script that can take an output layer and write a json file that specifies the architecture. Another script can read the json file and recreate the architecture. This works well when I also have a corresponding file with pretrained weights. However, if I don't have any pretrained weights there is currently no way to recover the initialization method of the parameters. 

A second use case of having this information available is inspecting training hyper parameters. I find it nice to write out a small file that contains performance metrics of the networks as well as hyperparameter details so I can compare hyperparameter settings. Manually remembering which models had which initializers can be a bit tedious. It would be nice to have a more automatic method.  

 After constructing an architecture most of the information needed to recreate it can be found by inspecting properties of the output_layer. Unfortunately this is not the case with the param initialization.

As a hack I've added these lines into lasagne/layers/base.py  
```

@@ -39,6 +39,7 @@ class Layer(object):
     def __init__(self, incoming, name=None):
         ...
         self.name = name
         self.params = OrderedDict()
+        self._initializers = OrderedDict()

     ...

     def get_params(self, unwrap_shared=True, **tags):
         ...
         tags['regularizable'] = tags.get('regularizable', True)
         self.params[param] = set(tag for tag, value in tags.items() if value)
+        from lasagne import init
+        if isinstance(spec, init.Initializer):
+            # Record initialization state
+            self._initializers[param] = spec

```

Adding a second ordered dictionary may not be the best way to add this feature. I'm aware of at least one case (in the batch normalization helper function) where the self.params dictionary is modified to remove a parameter. Using this implementation, modification would be needed anywhere a similar thing happened.  

Another implementation avenue might be to make a Param class that holds onto tags and other metadata, but that adds extra complexity to an implementation that is currently very nice and simple.  A third idea could be a single Params class to replace the ordered dictionary that holds onto metadata and implements the current dictionary interface for tags.  In my opinion, this third option sounds like the most elegant idea.

I'm not sure what the best trade-off for adding this feature would be, or if I'm the only one who would find it useful. I'd be interested in suggestions / feedback. 

-----

In summary I would like to account for the following two use cases: 
* Save layer parameter initialization metadata when writing architectures to a persistent format on disk. 
* Record how layers were initialized in training reports. 

I propose to replace the Layer params dictionary with a Params class that maintains the same tags interface. This class would maintain initialization and tags metadata for each parameter. Here is a quick mockup: 

````python

class Params(object):
    def __init__(self):
        # Store metadata about layer parameters
        self._tags = OrderedDict() 
        self._initializers = OrderedDict() 

    def keys(self):
        return self._tags.keys()

    def __getitem__(self, key):
        return self._tags[key]

    def __setitem__(self, key, val):
        self._tags[key] = val

    def __delitem__(self, key):
        del self._tags[key]
        del self._initializers[key]

````

There would likely be a bit more in the class (especially if **self.params is ever used), but these cases can be accounted for and this snippet illustrates the idea.

issue_title: Should we allow non-execution of cells for nbval?
issue_body:
We have a keyword `#NBVAL_IGNORE_OUTPUT` (see issue #16) that stops nbval from comparing the computed output against the stored output for the input cell in which the string `#NBVAL_IGNORE_OUTPUT` is found. However, the input cell code is still executed. This is normally useful as later cells may depend on it.

Would it be useful to also support another feature, maybe using the keyword `#NBVAL_IGNORE`, which means that the input cell in which this is found will not be executed at all? This could be used in cases where we know that the execution causes side effects on the host that executes the command, which we may not want or need as part of the nbval testing.

(u'Zipfile filenames:', ['\xd0\xa4\xd0\xb0\xd0\xb9\xd0\xbb'])
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "analyze_archive.py", line 16, in <module>
    with zip_fs.openbin(path) as handle:
  File "/home/fresheed/work/fix_registration/pyfilesystem2-master/fs/zipfs.py", line 371, in openbin
    return _ZipExtFile(self, zip_name)
  File "/home/fresheed/work/fix_registration/pyfilesystem2-master/fs/zipfs.py", line 33, in __init__
    self._end = _zip.getinfo(name).file_size
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/zipfile.py", line 905, in getinfo
    'There is no item named %r in the archive' % name)
KeyError: "There is no item named u'\\u0424\\u0430\\u0439\\u043b' in the archive"
```

As we can see, zipfile accepts filenames as bytestrings, while Pyfilesystem operates with Unicode. 
Probable solution is:
```
class _ZipExtFile(RawWrapper):

    def __init__(self, fs, name):
        self._zip = _zip = fs._zip
        name=name.encode(fs.encoding) #convert filename using parent fs's encoding
        self._end = _zip.getinfo(name).file_size
        self._pos = 0
        super(_ZipExtFile, self).__init__(_zip.open(name), 'r', name)
```
but I don't sure if it is the only place to change.

issue_title: Issue when setting playerargs with mpv
issue_body:
I am trying to set playerargs for mpv, let's say I want the video output to libcaca so I must set 

    --vo=caca

I see two ways of doing this:
1. With quotes:

       set playerargs "--vo=caca"

(or the equivalents "-vo=caca", -"-vo caca", "-vo caca" etc.)

What happens is that mpv thinks the arguement is a file, so I have the message "cannot open file "--vo=caca" if I omit the --really-quiet option and, of course, no libcaca output.

2. Without quotes:

        set playerargs --vo=caca

(or the equivalents -vo=caca, --vo caca, -vo caca etc.)

What is happening now is that the video plays as supposed,  i.e. with libcaca output (if i freeze at this point with pdb), BUT after opening the mpv subprocess an error is raised when sending the socket to mprisctl ([main.py, L1262](https://github.com/mps-youtube/mps-youtube/blob/develop/mps_youtube/main.py#L1261)). The error states:

*Problem playing last item: Expecting property name enclosed in double quotes: line 1 column 62 (char 61)*


Any Ideas or suggestions on how to fix this?

S.

Hi again,

Sorry for yet another feature request - I really appreciate all the recent improvements that have been made!

So when using the new multi-dataset mode in ganga athena, it stores the outputs under subdirectories according to the name of the text file that contained the input files. It would be nice if an option could be added to mirror what can happen in pathena:

https://twiki.cern.ch/twiki/bin/view/AtlasProtected/AthAnalysisBase#How_to_submit_your_job_to_the_gr

Specifically, can you add an option "--addNthFieldOfInDSToLFN=<N>", whereby if it is specified, the <N>th field of the text file name (fields separated by dot) is inserted into the name of the output file, and then the output files should no longer be put into separate directories, they should all just go into the output directory: the user will be able to use their selected field(s) to sort the output files how they wish.

This option should be able to take a list of numbers, and each specified field is added to the output filename. 

If this option isn't specified, the current behaviour can remain: outputs organized into subdirectories.

Many thanks
Will

Hi all,

I recently tried installing ```ganga``` on a well known OS which doesn't have case sensitivity turned on in it's filesystems by default.
(This was a quick exercise to get an estimate if we could support the platform).

As a result git and/or pip install becomes extremely confused about ```Ganga/__init__.py``` vs ```ganga/__init__.py```.

I know we're not aiming to support these environments but just before we go too far down the rabbit hole I'm curious to ask if we may want to reconsider the naming of ```ganga```?

I'm happy if we want to officially decide to not support these systems but I'm curious if there is an opinion as to whether it would be nice to run our testsuite elsewhere to help debug?

Thanks,

Rob

issue_title: RelatedFieldAjaxListFilter for m2m not working for Django < 1.9
issue_body:
RelatedFieldAjaxListFilter for m2m not working for Django < 1.9. It's because `field.remote_field.model` was introduced in Django 1.9. So you need to make some changes [here](https://github.com/geex-arts/django-jet/blob/master/jet/filters.py#L25).

```
from django.utils import version
from distutils.version import StrictVersion

def field_choices(self, field, request, model_admin):
    if StrictVersion(version.get_version()) >= StrictVersion('1.9'):
        model = (field.remote_field.model if hasattr(
                 field, 'remote_field') else field.related_field.model)
    else:
        model = (field.related_model if hasattr(
            field, 'related_model') else field.related_field.model)
```

issue_title: Fix HEAD request for content replay
issue_body:
When preforming a HEAD request on archived content, the request is canonicalized to look for HEAD request in the WARC, eg. to replay a HEAD request, instead of a more common 'GET request w/o payload' associated with HEAD requests.

Switch HEAD behavior to work as expected. If HEAD request lookup is really needed for replay, reevaluate, (eg. treat as replay of HEAD request if ajax?)

I'm curious about how others are setting up sync cables with multicamera arrangements. I currently have 4 Realsense D415 modules and I purchased all the necessary connectors and cables as stated in the multicamera white paper, but I don't see an obvious way to connect multiple grounds and SYNC lines together other than blobbing solder to join 4 crimp ends in a star configuration. issue_title: Should featurize_dataframe have an exceptions_encountered() variable?
issue_body:
Let's say you run featurize_dataframe with ignore_errors=True. After finishing featurization, you might want to just see what errors were encountered. If the BaseFeaturizer had an attribute/function called exceptions_encountered() perhaps it could return all the exceptions encountered during the previous featurize_dataframe() call.

Implementation would be simple - just create the variable, reset it at the beginning of the featurize_dataframe, and use traceback module to add the string version of each Exception to a list.

Another way to do it would be to add another parameter called ``return_errors`` to featurize_dataframe(). If ``ignore_errors`` and ``return_errors`` are both true, then featurize_dataframe would change its return type to be a tuple of the new dataframe and a list of errors encountered. This would avoid having a state variable for the previous errors.

Maybe other ways to do it - but the point would be to be able to see a list of errors encountered during featurize_dataframe with ignore_errors = True.

@WardLT 

See:
```
# TODO: implement citations
```
matminer.featurizers.composition.ElementFraction

I sometimes separate simulation (sim.py) and simulation-data-reading code (simdat.py) - that way I don't have to load the whole sim to read sim data. However, to properly analyze simulation data, certain data structures are needed (number of cells, cell IDs, cell types, etc.). Is it easy for users to add the key data structures to NetPyne structures that get saved? Probably...

Pulp Smash defines a variety of custom exceptions. This is appropriate: if a standard exception does not fit a situation well, one should define a new exception and raise it instead of executing `raise Exception`. The problem is that those custom exceptions are scattered throughout the codebase. Let's do the following:

1. Define a new module, `pulp_smash.exceptions`.
2. Move all custom exceptions into this module, and adjust references to those exceptions as necessary.
3. Ensure the various make recipes reference this new module. This means at least `make docs-html` and `make lint-pylint`.

issue_title: Interfaces with fields that are interfaces
issue_body:
Apologies if this is a little convoluted - it's hard to explain!

https://github.com/facebook/graphql/pull/123 allows implementations of interfaces to have fields that are implementations of interfaces. Currently `assert_object_implements_interface` (`graphql/core/type/schema.py`) only allows the fields to match types exactly. An example:

```
class InterfaceA(graphene.Interface):
    ...

class InterfaceB(graphene.Interface):
    my_field = graphene.List(InterfaceA)
    ...

class ImplementationA(InterfaceA):
    ...

class ImplementationB(InterfaceB):
    my_field = graphene.List(ImplementationA)
    ...
```

To test something out I made `assert_object_implements_interface` a no-op then hit further issues with `graphql/core/validation/rules/overlapping_fields_can_be_merged.py`. A simplified part of a query (in practise the query was built up from fragments coming from different places) returning an object of type ImplementationB:

```
my_field
... on InterfaceB {
    my_field
}
```

In theory it should be able to merge these, but in practice the first `my_field` is known to be `ImplementationA` and the second only `InterfaceA`.

I think the first part of this issue is worth fixing and I think I could probably manage that and open a PR if you'd like.

The second part I'm less sure about and I would have a harder time fixing - let me know what you think though. It's possible to work around the second problem by making sure queries are wrapped with `... on InterfaceB` when merging might happen.

Just getting started with vdirsyncer, starting with contacts. One of my first tests is a vcard like this:

```
BEGIN:VCARD
VERSION:3.0
UID:xu8wbtiol6loyvnm9hwpk697
fn:Sarah Miller
n:Miller;Sarah;
email;type=home;type=pref:sarahmiller@foo.com
REV:20161115T061350Z
END:VCARD
```

Change on side A:

```
fn:Sarah Millers
n:Millers;Sarah;
```

Change on side B:

```
FN:Sarah Miller
N:Miller;Sarah;;;
ORG:foo;
```

As expected, this error is returned:

```
error: my_contacts/Default: One item changed on both sides. Resolve this conflict manually, or by setting the `conflict_resolution` parameter in your config file.
```

Is it worth my time to implement a lower level diff, checking whether the changes can be resolved without forcing "A wins"? Looks like this could be implemented with `["command", "my_command"]`.

- I'm being pedantic; just use "A wins".
- Implementing in Python would be a helpful PR.
- This could be useful; implement as an external command and share elsewhere.

issue_title: Use account reuse symlink logic when loading an account
issue_body:
To reproduce, start with an empty Certbot config directory and use any released version of Certbot to obtain an ACMEv1 prod cert. You shouldn't set `--server` anywhere.

Then checkout the `acmev2-default` branch and run `certbot renew --force-renewal`. You should see an error like:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/bmw/src/certbot/certbot/renewal.py", line 430, in handle_renewal_request
    main.renew_cert(lineage_config, plugins, renewal_candidate)
  File "/home/bmw/src/certbot/certbot/main.py", line 1190, in renew_cert
    le_client = _init_le_client(config, auth, installer)
  File "/home/bmw/src/certbot/certbot/main.py", line 641, in _init_le_client
    acc, acme = _determine_account(config)
  File "/home/bmw/src/certbot/certbot/main.py", line 508, in _determine_account
    acc = account_storage.load(config.account)
  File "/home/bmw/src/certbot/certbot/account.py", line 224, in load
    return self._load_for_server_path(account_id, self.config.server_path)
  File "/home/bmw/src/certbot/certbot/account.py", line 204, in _load_for_server_path
    "Account at %s does not exist" % account_dir_path)
AccountNotFound: Account at /etc/letsencrypt/accounts/acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory/8f7e408abe6ff5832f20529fb812abad does not exist
```
This happens because the default server value changed to ACMEv2 and Certbot is looking for the account specified in the renewal configuration file in the ACMEv2 account directory which doesn't exist and hasn't been symlinked yet.

## My operating system is (include version):
Ubuntu 14.04.5 LTS 

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
It was executed from source `git clone https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt`

## I ran this command and it produced this output:
```
./letsencrypt-auto renew --agree-tos
Error: couldn't get currently installed version for /root/.local/share/letsencrypt/bin/letsencrypt:

issue_title: Disallow priv key sharing between two local running raiden nodes and remove nonce management
issue_body:
From [this](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/pull/1392#issuecomment-387089813) comment.



>Either we assume that we share the privatekey or not:
>
>- if we don't share it, then the nonce update is useless: The running Raiden node is the only one  sending transactions with that key, therefor the only node changing the nonce, so the local nonce is always valid.
>   - assuming the ethereum node puts transactions in pending *faster* than Raiden restarts.
>- if we do share, then there **will be** races where two nodes send the different transactions with the  same nonce, only updating the nonce without fixing and resending the transactions is not sufficient. At  the best case scenario, the retry logic would also fix the transaction, but then either:
>  - the two nodes deadlock each other and waste ether, because one will try to overwrite the other   transaction and needs to pay more for the gas
>  - the node which sent the rejected transaction accepts that it lost the race and sends a new  transaction with a fresher nonce. This is IMO just too annoying, the effort is not worth and would be better to just /not/ use local signing.

@hackaugusto Did I understand correctly that you want to disallow using the same private key in 2 local running raiden nodes? And with that assumption throw away the nonce management?

issue_title: resume task fails on non-submitting machine.
issue_body:
Currently resume is basically a resubmission of job, which generates job, transfer to remote host, and start. This make it impossible to resume a task from non-submitting machine on which the original task definition file `.def` does not exist. 

I suppose we can make resume a real resume in the sense that we simply resubmit the job on the remote host.

issue_title: List parameters and their annotations in `global` section with `-h` option
issue_body:
So suppose I have this workflow:

```
$ sos run workflows/fastqtl_to_mash.ipynb -h -v3


Available workflows
  export
  default
  convert

Sections
  convert_0:           Generate utility functions
  convert_1:           Convert summary stats gzip format to HDF5
  convert_2:           Merge single study data to multivariate data
  default_3:           Extract data to fit MASH model
  default_4:           Compute for MASH EZ model and save to RDS
  export:              Export notebook to HTML file
```

it neatly lists all steps in the workflow. But under `[global]` I have something like this:

```
parameter: cwd = path('./gtex6_workflow_output')
parameter: data_list = path("data/eQTL_summary_files.txt")
parameter: gene_list = path()
parameter: msg = "GTEx eQTL summary statistics"
# maximum number of groups per HDF5 file
parameter: maxsize = 1000 
parameter: cols = [8, 9, 7]
parameter: keep_ensg_version = 0
# number of null samples per gene to train MASH mixture
parameter: null_per_gene = 9 
# a list of effect names (SNP names) to exclude from mash analysis
parameter: exclude_mash_effects_list = 'NULL' 
# a list of condition names (tissue names) to include in mash output. 
# Default includes all conditions.
parameter: include_mash_condition_list = 'NULL' 
# size of MASH train set
parameter: mash_train_size = 20000 
```

Correct me if I am wrong, but I thought we can list parameters with `-v`? I do not think it works, though. It would be great if we can list parameters along with what they are, eg:

```
--exclude_mash_effects_list: a list of effect names (SNP names) to exclude from mash analysis
```

Not sure if `#` is the best identifier for parameter comments to show, but we can also do `##` or `#'` (`roxygen` convention). I'm just saying that showing parameter options could be useful when I make quick software-type of workflows for others to run without looking into the details.

issue_title: Clean up cancellation logic throughout the engine.
issue_body:
We take a variety of cancellation approaches in the engine, including:
- `Close` methods
- `Cancel` methods
- `util/cancel` contexts
- `context.Contexl`

We should look through these carefully. I _think_ it's reasonable to expect that we can unify on `util/cancel` for any component that needs to observe two-level cancellation and `context.Context` for everything else, but I'm not certain.

In addition to the varied approaches, it is not clear where the responsibility lies for shutting down various components. We should clarify this as well.

issue_title: Jaeger traces outside of Pulumi CLI invocation
issue_body:
I'm trying to debug something, and noticing that we emit `pulumi-cli: /pulumirpc.Engine/Log` traces outside of the context of a CLI invocation.

I would expect all of these separate traces to be within the context of a single CLI invocation, i.e. parented under the `<trace-without-root-span>`. 

That "without root span" trace eventually gets named when the command completes, but it isn't especially descriptive. It's just `pulumi-cli: pulumi`. Ideally we could include the command name, e.g. `pulumi-cli: update` or `pulumi-cli: stack-rm`.

    else:        
        demand_diff = float(err_distribution_part["demand_qty"].max())

    for t in range(test_len):
        this_day = pd.to_datetime(starting_test) + relativedelta(days=t)
        day_list.append(this_day)
        predict_lt_demand = avg_moving_forecast(lt=lt, move_len=30, out_inv_df=in_out_inv_df,
                                                this_day=this_day)
        if t >= lt:
            out_end_date = this_day - relativedelta(days=1)
            out_start_date = out_end_date - relativedelta(days=lt - 1)
            actual_lt_demand = in_out_inv_df[(in_out_inv_df["inv_date"] >= out_start_date) &
                                             (in_out_inv_df["inv_date"] <= out_end_date)]["out_qty"].sum()
            error_lt_demand = actual_lt_demand - predict_lt_demand
            if error_lt_demand >= 0:
                lt_demand_diff = lt_demand_diff.append(pd.Series([error_lt_demand], [this_day]))
                lt_demand_diff = lt_demand_diff[1:]
                
                err_distribution_df = cal_err_distribution(lt_demand_diff=lt_demand_diff)
                err_distribution_part = err_distribution_df[err_distribution_df["alpha"] <= opt_alpha]
                if len(err_distribution_df) == 1 or err_distribution_part.empty:
                    demand_diff = float(err_distribution_df["demand_qty"].min())
                else:
                    
                    demand_diff = float(err_distribution_part["demand_qty"].max())
        item_safety_stock = cal_safe_stock_v60(inv_out_df=in_out_inv_df, point_date=this_day)
        this_day_inv = i_real.get(this_day, 0)
        i_i.append(this_day_inv)
        item_lt_demand = predict_lt_demand + demand_diff
        vendor_demand = (fixed_days / lt) * item_lt_demand
        safety_stock_list.append(item_safety_stock)
        
        lt_demand_list.append(item_lt_demand)
        vendor_demand_list.append(vendor_demand)
        i_next = sum(q_negative1) + i0 - item_lt_demand
        if i_next < item_safety_stock:
            q_day = vendor_demand + item_safety_stock - i_next
            q_day = math.ceil(q_day / pickup_qty) * pickup_qty
           
            if min_qty > 0 and q_day < min_qty:
                q_day = math.ceil(min_qty / pickup_qty) * pickup_qty
            if q_day == 0:
                q_day = pickup_qty
        else:
            q_day = 0
        q_list.append(q_day)
        if t < lt:
            i0 = q_negative1[0] + i0 - test[t]
        else:
            i0 = q_negative1[0] + i0 - test[t] + item_input[t]
        if i0 < 0:
            i0 = 0
        i_i1.append(i0)
        del q_negative1[0]
        q_negative1.append(q_day)
    log_file = pd.DataFrame(
        columns=["date", "real demand", "Q", "real I", "optimized I", "safety_stock", "L_demand",
                 "vendor_demand"])
    i_i_len = len(i_i)
    for m in range(i_i_len):
        log_file.loc[m] = [day_list[m], test[m], q_list[m], i_i[m], i_i1[m], safety_stock_list[m],
                           lt_demand_list[m], vendor_demand_list[m]]
    log_file["item"] = item
    log_file["lt"] = lt
    return log_file


class SimulationGlobalParams(object):
    def __init__(self, company, cid):     
        self.train_time_len = int(model_config.get("train_time_len") or 3)
        self.company = company
        self.cid = cid
        self.low_alpha = 0
        
        max_inv_date = db_layer.max_inv_date(company=company, cid=cid)
        Logger.info("max_inventory_date: {}".format(max_inv_date))
        
        self.test_end_date = (pd.to_datetime(max_inv_date) - relativedelta(days=1)).date()     
        self.test_start_date = self.test_end_date - relativedelta(months=self.train_time_len)       
        self.train_end_date = self.test_start_date - relativedelta(days=1)      
        self.train_start_date = self.train_end_date - relativedelta(months=self.train_time_len)           
        self.his_start_date = self.test_end_date - relativedelta(months=3 * self.train_time_len)     
        self.his_end_date = pd.to_datetime(max_inv_date).date()   
        self.move_len = 30    
        self.cal_diff_len = 3


@ray.remote
def train(params, item_lt_list_df, item_property_df, per_item_df, series):
    item_id, item_df = per_item_df
    item = series[item_id]
    print(item_id, item)
    lt = int(round(item_lt_list_df[item_lt_list_df["item"] == item]["lt_list"].mean())) or 1
    lt_list = item_lt_list_df[item_lt_list_df["item"] == item]["lt_list"].tolist()
    item_property = item_property_df[item_property_df["item"] == item]
   
    if item_property["pickup_qty"].empty:
        pickup_qty = 1
    else:
        pickup_qty = list(item_property["pickup_qty"])[0] or 1
        
    if item_property["fixed_days_supply"].empty:
        fixed_days = 1
    else:
        fixed_days = item_property["fixed_days_supply"].tolist()[0] or 1
    
    train_q_negative = list(item_df[item_df["inv_date"] >= params.train_start_date]["arrive_qty"])[:lt]
    
    test_q_negative = list(item_df[item_df["inv_date"] >= params.test_start_date]["arrive_qty"])[:lt]
    alpha_df = item_init_gamma(
        params=params,
        item=item,
        lt=lt,
        lt_list=lt_list,
        q_negative=train_q_negative,
        pickup_qty=pickup_qty,
        fixed_days=fixed_days,
        in_out_inv_df=item_df)
    if alpha_df.empty:
        return pd.DataFrame(), pd.DataFrame()
    else:
        item_log_file = item_use_gamma(
            item=item,
            lt=lt,
            lt_list=lt_list,
            q_negative=test_q_negative,
            pickup_qty=pickup_qty,
            alpha_df=alpha_df,
            fixed_days=fixed_days,
            in_out_inv_df=item_df,
            per_item_property=item_property,
            params=params)
        return alpha_df, item_log_file


if __name__ == "__main__":
    #redis_address="192.168.17.19:61588"
    ray.init()
    tic = time.time()
    run()
    toc = time.time()
    print(float(toc - tic), "seconds")
